summary: I grew up around Lynch, Kentucky, where U.S. Steel operated the mines. It was a coal mining town, and I attended Lynch High School there, graduating in 1974. Later, I worked in the mines in Lynch starting in 1977, first for U.S. Steel and then for Arch Coal after they took over. Lynch was a central place in my life, both for education and work.

I spent my childhood in Benham, Kentucky, during the 1940s and 1950s. It was a mining community similar to Lynch, and many of my classmates worked in the mines there or in Lynch. Benham was part of the coal mining culture that shaped my early years and influenced my later decision to work in the mines.

I attended Lynch High School and graduated in 1974. Before that, I went to what they called East West School for elementary, which was integrated before I moved on to high school. My brother and sister also went to Lynch High School, so it was a family tradition. The school was a big part of my youth in Lynch, Kentucky.

I was interviewed by CNN about my experience as a female coal miner. Bill Turner invited me to do a luncheon and then an interview at the East Kentucky Social Club, where I shared my story. It was a significant moment because it gave me a platform to talk about breaking barriers in the mining industry.

I started working as a coal miner for U.S. Steel in 1977, shortly after having my son. U.S. Steel was the original company running the mines in Lynch, Kentucky. I worked there until they sold the mines to Arch Coal, and it was with U.S. Steel that I first entered the mining workforce as a woman.

After U.S. Steel sold the mines, Arch Coal took over, and I continued working there until I was laid off around 1983. Arch Coal became the new operator of the mines in Lynch, Kentucky, and I experienced the transition firsthand. My time with Arch Coal marked the latter part of my mining career.

Chicago was where my family was, and one of my motivations for working in the mines was to earn enough money to visit them. After I started working in 1977, I planned to save money to go to Chicago. It was a goal that helped me push through the challenges of being a female miner in Kentucky.

Shirley Smith was one of the women miners who worked in the mines before I started. She was from the area and part of the small group of female coal miners breaking into the industry. I know she still lives near Lynch, in Cumberland, and I hope to talk to her more about her experiences someday.

Harlan, Kentucky, was home to one of the female miners I knew, though I can't recall her name now. She was part of the early group of women working in the mines alongside Shirley Smith and Marcella Boggs. Harlan was another coal mining community connected to my story through these women.

Marcella Boggs was another woman who worked in the mines before I started in 1977. Like Shirley Smith, she was part of the pioneering group of female miners in the Lynch area. She still lives nearby, and I hope to ask her about her experiences as a woman in mining when I see her next.

The East Kentucky Social Club is where I did my CNN interview about being a female coal miner. It's an important community space, especially for African American and Black residents in the area. The club provided a setting for me to share my story and connect with others about the challenges and progress in mining.
